title: Tofacitinib -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
content: Continuous communication between the interprofessional team, primarily the PCP and specialist, and their patients is imperative, and thorough education on treatment and disease should be provided. Updates on the latest guidelines on disease management using tofacitinib should be routinely reviewed. The patients should be advised to report any new-onset clinical manifestations. Period follow-ups visits with the PCP and specialist are essential to prevent complications, hospitalization, and mortality.

title: Tofacitinib -- Indications
content: Tofacitinib's indication as a treatment for ulcerative colitis (UC) was approved by the FDA following three randomized phase III placebo-controlled clinical trials (OCTAVE Sustain trial, OCTAVE Induction 1, OCTAVE Induction 2), which were conducted following a promising phase 2 trial. [4] The agent is the first medication in the JAK inhibitors class of drugs to be approved in the UC management. [5] Similar to RA and PA, tofacitinib's indication in UC is for adult patients with active moderate or severe disease who have not demonstrated an adequate response to TNF blockers or intolerance to therapy.

title: Tofacitinib -- Indications
content: Janus kinases are phosphotransferases, and receptor engagement by cytokines activates their enzymatic function. Janus kinase inhibitors represent a new strategy for the treatment of immune and inflammatory diseases. Tofacitinib is a targeted synthetic small molecule that is an oral inhibitor of Janus kinases (JAKs). It is (JAK) inhibitor FDA-approved in April 2012 and indicated for its use in the management of rheumatoid arthritis (RA), psoriatic arthritis (PA), ulcerative colitis (UC), polyarticular course juvenile idiopathic arthritis (pcJIA). [1] Tofacitinib's action on the nonreceptor tyrosine kinase JAK enzymes is more preferential to the JAK-1 and JAK-3 enzymes. [1]

title: Upadacitinib -- Indications
content: The American Academy of Dermatology (AAD) endorses using upadacitinib for adults with moderate to severe atopic dermatitis (AD). Upadacitinib is FDA-approved for patients with AD who have failed other systemic therapies, including biologics, or when the use of those therapies is inadvisable. [4] For patients with ulcerative colitis (UC) who experience recurrent pouchitis after ileal pouch-anal anastomosis (IPAA), the American Gastroenterological Association (AGA) suggests advanced immunosuppressive therapies like upadacitinib. Upadacitinib can be considered for chronic antibiotic-dependent pouchitis. Endoscopic evaluation is advised to confirm inflammation and rule out other causes. For patients with ulcerative colitis (UC) who have undergone IPAA and develop symptoms due to cuffitis, the AGA suggests using topical therapies approved for UC treatment, such as topical mesalamine and topical corticosteroids. Upadacitinib or other immunosuppressive therapies may be considered for refractory cases. [5] According to the American College of Rheumatology guidelines, targeted synthetic disease-modifying antirheumatic drugs (tsDMARDs), including JAK inhibitors like tofacitinib, baricitinib, and upadacitinib, are recommended as part of a treat-to-target (TTT) approach. This approach is strongly recommended over standard care for patients without prior treatment with biologic DMARDs (bDMARDs) or tsDMARDs. This recommendation extends to optimizing methotrexate dosage and subsequently adding DMARDs as needed. Although the evidence is of low certainty, this recommendation is maintained due to the recognized importance of systematic monitoring and treatment adjustment to minimize inflammation, thereby preventing joint damage and other long-term complications such as cardiovascular disease and osteoporosis. In DMARD-naive patients with moderate-to-high disease activity, methotrexate monotherapy is recommended. [6] The indication algorithm for rheumatoid arthritis is provided in Image. Upadacitinib for Rheumatoid Arthritis Algorithm. [7]

[3] The trials included subjects with psoriatic arthritis receiving tofacitinib treatment with a 5 mg dosage twice a day in sequence with MTX or other conventional synthetic DMARD therapy. Similar to RA, it is indicated for psoriatic arthritis in adult patients unresponsive or developed interlace to one or more disease-modifying rheumatoid drugs (DMARDs) with an active moderate to severe psoriatic disease course.

title: Tofacitinib -- Toxicity
content: Tofacitinib therapy in females of reproductive age may cause a possible risk to a developing fetus during pregnancy and may also impair infertility in women of reproductive potential. Females who are lactating and receiving treatment are advised not to breastfeed 18 to 36 hours following the last dosage.

title: Tofacitinib -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
content: Tofacitinib is a Janus kinase (JAK) inhibitor FDA-approved in April 2012 indicated for its use in the management of rheumatoid arthritis (RA), psoriatic arthritis (PA), ulcerative colitis (UC), and polyarticular course juvenile idiopathic arthritis (pcJIA). Patients suffering from such ailments require oversight from an interprofessional team of healthcare professionals. The interprofessional team can include a primary care physician (PCP), a rheumatologist, gastroenterologist, mid-level practitioners, nurses, a physical therapist (PT), and a pharmacist. Efficient interprofessional care from the healthcare team of patients suffering from RA, PA, or UC can lead to clinical and symptomatic improvements and a better quality of life. [Level 5]

title: Baricitinib -- Contraindications -- Box Warnings
content: Major adverse cardiovascular events: Rheumatoid arthritis patients aged 50 or older with at least one cardiovascular risk factor and treated with other JAK inhibitors have a higher frequency of major adverse cardiovascular events (MACE) than those treated with TNF blockers. Patients with a history of smoking have an increased risk of MACE. In cases of myocardial infarction or stroke, baricitinib treatment should be discontinued. Thrombosis: Patients treated with baricitinib have an increased risk of developing DVT, pulmonary embolism, and arterial thrombosis, which can lead to severe and fatal consequences. [24] [25]

title: Baricitinib -- Continuing Education Activity
content: Baricitinib is a disease-modifying antirheumatic drug (DMARD) indicated for rheumatoid arthritis when conventional DMARDs prove inadequate. This medication is approved by the U.S. Food and Drug Administration (FDA) to treat adult patients with moderately to severely active rheumatoid arthritis who have not responded adequately to other DMARDs, including tumor necrosis factor antagonist therapies. Furthermore, baricitinib is FDA-approved for treating COVID-19, presenting a valuable choice in combination with other therapies for hospitalized patients, including pediatric cases necessitating supplemental oxygen, invasive mechanical ventilation, or extracorporeal membrane oxygenation. This activity comprehensively reviews the indications, contraindications, mechanisms, adverse events, and other crucial aspects of baricitinib therapy within the clinical setting. This activity is tailored to meet the specific needs of an interprofessional healthcare team responsible for treating patients with rheumatoid arthritis, alopecia areata, and COVID-19.

title: Tofacitinib -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
content: The interprofessional team should initiate a baseline laboratory workup prior to starting tofacitinib therapy. Patients considering tofacitinib treatment should be checked for active or latent TB, CBC, LFT, and lipid panel primarily and routinely after that. Women of reproductive potential should be counseled on the risk of possible infertility from tofacitinib, and treatment during pregnancy may increase potential risk to the fetus and discontinue breastfeeding as tofacitinib may be excreted in breastmilk. Regular communications and follow-ups between patients and their health providers build reliance and rapport, strengthening medication compliance, further reducing the progression of ailments while enhancing the quality of life.

title: Alopecia Totalis -- Treatment / Management -- JAK Inhibitors
content: JAK inhibitors are newer medications that are currently being studied in alopecia areata and totalis. These include tofacitinib, ruxolitinib, and baricitinib. These agents have promising therapeutic benefits due to our current understanding of the pathogenesis of AA. JAK inhibitors have proven efficacious in treating other inflammatory conditions such as rheumatoid arthritis and psoriasis. There are a few open-label trials for AA patients, which have shown impressive results with significant hair regrowth. However, many patients have a recurrence of hair loss after therapy discontinuation. Side effects of oral JAK inhibitors include viral reactivation, infection, transaminitis, and bone marrow suppression. [12] JAK inhibitors have also been used topically. [18] Topical 2% tofacitinib has been reported to have good results, though relapse upon discontinuation is also high. [21]

One randomized post-marketing safety trial studied the effect of a Jak inhibitor in patients with rheumatoid arthritis (RA). title: UMLS -- Run 14 -- Level 3 -- Community 1485
content: Immunosuppressants constitute a broad therapeutic class used to dampen immune activity in settings such as organ transplantation, autoimmune disorders, and inflammatory diseases. This class includes agents that act through diverse mechanisms, ranging from inhibition of interleukin signaling to blockade of calcineurin or mTOR pathways and direct depletion of lymphocytes. Representative members of the immunosuppressant family are inhibitory interleukinu (agents that inhibit interleukin activity), mycophenolate, and mykofenolát mofetil (mycophenolate mofetil). Additional compounds classified as immunosuppressants are antilymfocytární sérum (antilymphocyte serum), azaserin, basiliximab, cyklosporiny (cyclosporine), daklizumab (daclizumab), elipticiny, everolimus, gliotoxin, and hydrochlorid mykofenolátu mofetilu (the hydrochloride form of mycophenolate mofetil). Specific immunosuppressive drugs also highlighted within this group are kyselina mykofenolová (mycophenolic acid), muromonab‑CD3, and takrolimus (tacrolimus). By recognizing the wide array of immunosuppressants and their distinct pharmacologic actions, clinicians can tailor immunomodulatory regimens to achieve optimal graft survival, control autoimmune activity, and minimize adverse effects.

content: Immunosuppressants are a class of immunologic factors that act to dampen or inhibit the activity of the immune system. title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 2 -- Community 329
content: Immunosuppressants constitute a broad class of immunologic agents that modulate the immune response. This class includes specific drugs such as inhibitory interleukinu, mycophenolate, mykofenolát mofetil, antilymfocytární sérum, azaserin, basiliximab, cyklosporiny (cyclosporine), daklizumab, elipticiny, everolimus, gliotoxin, hydrochlorid mykofenolátu mofetilu, as well as related compounds koformycin, kyselina mykofenolová (mycophenolic acid), muromonab‑CD3, and takrolimus. The biosynthetic pathways for antilymfocytární sérum, azaserin, and basiliximab are characterized and can be described in detail. Biosynthesis, a fundamental biological process, can be qualified or modulated by numerous substances, including the amino acid cystein, the plant alkaloids protoveratriny, natural quinoline alkaloids elipticiny, the fungal metabolite gliotoxin, simple trisaccharides, and immunological factors (imunologické faktory). Immunosuppressive agents such as cyklosporiny and daklizumab influence biosynthetic activity in immune cells, while inhibitory interleukinu suppresses biosynthesis by down‑regulating immune signaling. Koformycin serves as a probe of biosynthetic enzyme function, and muromonab‑CD3 modulates T‑cell activation, thereby affecting biosynthetic pathways. The anabolic steroid testosteronpropionát impacts protein‑synthetic biosynthetic routes. Collectively, these entities illustrate the hierarchical organization of immunosuppressive therapies, their diverse chemical forms, and their integration with biosynthetic processes, highlighting their relevance for research, diagnostics, and therapeutic manipulation.

Deflazacort, prednison, fluocinonid, diflorasone, halcinonid, and triamcinolonacetonid are all members of the antiflogistika group. Each of these compounds exerts anti‑inflammatory effects through glucocorticoid receptor activation, leading to suppression of pro‑inflammatory cytokines and immune cell activity. Recognizing that these medications belong to the same therapeutic class helps clinicians select appropriate anti‑inflammatory therapy, anticipate similar side‑effect profiles, and manage dosing strategies across conditions such as allergic reactions, autoimmune disorders, and dermatologic inflammation.

title: UMLS -- Run 33 -- Level 3 -- Community 2127
content: Immunosuppressants constitute a diverse group of agents that dampen immune activity and are employed in transplantation, autoimmune diseases, and other conditions requiring immune modulation. This class includes inhibitory interleukin compounds, the antimetabolite mycophenolate and its pro‑drug mycophenolate mofetil, antilymphocyte serum, azaserin, the monoclonal antibodies basiliximab and daclizumab, the mTOR inhibitor everolimus, and the fungal toxin gliotoxin. Additional members are cyclophosphamide (koformycin), mycophenolic acid (kyselina mykofenolová), the glucocorticoid paramethasone, and the calcineurin inhibitor tacrolimus. These drugs act through various mechanisms—such as blocking interleukin signaling, inhibiting nucleotide synthesis, antagonizing the IL‑2 receptor, inhibiting mTOR signaling, or modulating glucocorticoid and calcineurin pathways—to achieve immunosuppression and improve patient outcomes in settings where controlling the immune response is critical.

title: UMLS -- Run 11 -- Level 3 -- Community 1471
content: Immunosuppressants are a category of pharmacologic agents that belong to the broader group of immunological factors and are used to diminish or modulate the activity of the immune system. Within this class, mycophenolate and mycophenolate mofetil are identified as child entities, indicating that they are specific forms or derivatives of immunosuppressants. A wide variety of individual drugs are recognized as examples of immunosuppressants, including azaserin, cyclosporine, daclizumab, ellipticines, everolimus, gliotoxin, and the hydrochloride salt of mycophenolate mofetil. Additional agents that fall under the immunosuppressant umbrella are koformycin, mycophenolic acid (kyselina mykofenolová), muromonab‑CD3, paramethasone, and tacrolimus. Together, these substances illustrate the diverse chemical families and mechanisms that can be employed to achieve immunosuppression, highlighting their relevance in transplant medicine, autoimmune disease management, and other clinical scenarios where controlling immune activity is essential.

Within this therapeutic class, mycophenolate is identified as a subordinate or child category, reflecting its specific role among immunosuppressants. Azathioprine sodium additionally functions as a purine antagonist, highlighting its mechanism of inhibiting purine synthesis. title: UMLS -- Run 34 -- Level 3 -- Community 2595
content: Immunosuppressants represent a broad pharmacologic class that includes a variety of agents used to dampen immune activity. Within this class, interleukin‑inhibitory compounds are listed as child entities, alongside the antimetabolite mycophenolate and its pro‑drug form mycophenolate mofetil (mykofenolát mofetil). Additional immunosuppressive agents identified as members of this class are antilymphocyte serum (antilymfocytární sérum), the monoclonal antibodies basiliximab and daclizumab (daklizumab), the mTOR inhibitor everolimus, the fungal toxin gliotoxin, and the salt form hydrochloride of mycophenolate mofetil (hydrochlorid mykofenolátu mofetilu). The class also explicitly encompasses the drugs mycophenolic acid (kyselina mykofenolová), cyclophosphamide (koformycin), and tacrolimus (takrolimus). Together, these entities illustrate the diverse mechanisms—ranging from cytokine blockade and lymphocyte depletion to inhibition of nucleotide synthesis and mTOR signaling—by which immunosuppressants achieve therapeutic immune modulation in transplantation, autoimmune disease, and other clinical settings.

title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 3 -- Community 1400
content: Immunosuppressants represent a diverse pharmacologic class that includes antilymphocyte serum, azaserine, cyclosporine, daclizumab, ellipticins, everolimus, gliotoxin, mycophenolic acid (kyselina mykofenolová), muromonab‑CD3, and tacrolimus (takrolimus). Within this class, inhibitory interleukin agents are considered a subordinate subgroup (“has child”). These agents act to dampen immune responses and are employed in conditions requiring suppression of lymphocyte activity, such as organ transplantation, autoimmune diseases, and certain inflammatory disorders. However, the use of immunosuppressants is contraindicated in the setting of human herpesvirus 1 (HSV‑1) infection, reflecting the heightened risk of viral reactivation when immune function is deliberately reduced. Recognizing both the therapeutic breadth of immunosuppressants and their specific contraindications is essential for safe patient management and optimal clinical outcomes.

title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 2 -- Community 1242
content: Immunosuppressants constitute a major subgroup of immunological factors and include a wide variety of agents that act to dampen immune activity in clinical settings such as organ transplantation and autoimmune disease. Within this class, inhibitory interleukinu, mycophenolate and mykofenolát mofetil are identified as child categories of immunosuppressants, reflecting their specific mechanistic focus on cytokine inhibition and purine synthesis blockade. A broad spectrum of individual drugs and biologics are classified as immunosuppressants, including antilymfocytární sérum, azaserin, basiliximab, cyklosporiny, daklizumab, elipticiny, everolimus, gliotoxin, hydrochlorid mykofenolátu mofetilu, koformycin, kyselina mykofenolová, muromonab‑CD3 and takrolimus. These agents encompass diverse mechanisms such as monoclonal antibody‑mediated lymphocyte depletion (e.g., antilymfocytární sérum, muromonab‑CD3, basiliximab, daklizumab), inhibition of calcineurin signaling (cyclosporine, takrolimus), blockade of mTOR pathways (everolimus), interference with nucleotide synthesis (mycophenolate, mykofenolát mofetil, azaserin, koformycin, kyselina mykofenolová), and other cytotoxic or immunomodulatory effects (gliotoxin, elipticiny). By grouping these compounds under the umbrella of immunosuppressants, clinicians can appreciate their shared therapeutic goal of reducing immune‑mediated damage while recognizing the distinct pharmacologic profiles that guide individualized patient care.

title: UMLS -- Run 21 -- Level 3 -- Community 1690
content: Immunosuppressants comprise a diverse group of agents that are used to dampen immune activity in settings such as organ transplantation, autoimmune disorders, and inflammatory diseases. The class includes mycophenolate and its pro‑drug mykofenolát mofetil (mycophenolate mofetil), as well as the hydrochloride form of mykofenolát mofetilu, which all act by inhibiting inosine monophosphate dehydrogenase and thereby reducing lymphocyte proliferation. Kyselina mykofenolová (mycophenolic acid) is the active acid form of this pathway and is likewise classified as an immunosuppressant. Takrolimus (tacrolimus) and koformycin are calcineurin‑inhibiting and nucleoside‑analog agents, respectively, that suppress T‑cell activation through distinct molecular mechanisms. Inhibitory interleukinu (interleukin inhibitors) represent biologic immunosuppressants that block cytokine signaling pathways, while daclizumab (daklizumab) is a monoclonal antibody targeting the IL‑2 receptor to prevent lymphocyte expansion. Antilymfocytární sérum (antilymphocyte serum) provides polyclonal antibodies that deplete circulating lymphocytes, and azaserin is another antibody‑based agent with immunosuppressive properties. Elipticiny (ellipticins) and gliotoxin are natural‑product‑derived compounds that exhibit immunosuppressive activity through mechanisms such as inhibition of NF‑κB signaling and induction of oxidative stress. Together, these agents—mycophenolate, mykofenolát mofetil, hydrochlorid mykofenolátu mofetilu, kyselina mykofenolová, takrolimus, koformycin, inhibitory interleukinu, daclizumab, antilymfocytární sérum, azaserin, elipticiny, and gliotoxin—form a comprehensive pharmacologic arsenal for controlling undesirable immune responses, each offering specific advantages and considerations for individualized patient care.

title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 2 -- Community 357
content: Biosynthesis, the cellular process of constructing complex molecules from simpler precursors, can be qualified or modulated by a diverse set of agents—including the antibiotic‑like compound mepartricin, the amino acid cystein, the neurosteroid pregnanolone, the prostacyclin analog epoprostenol, the alkaloid protoveratriny, the quinone glaukarubin, the ionophore lasalocid, the steroid ester testosteronpropionát, and simple carbohydrates such as trisacharidy—as well as by several immunologically active substances such as cyklosporiny, daklizumab, elipticiny, gliotoxin, koformycin, muromonab‑CD3, and inhibitory interleukins, each of which influences specific enzymatic, signaling, or ion‑dependent steps of metabolic pathways. Many of these qualifiers are themselves immunosuppressive agents; the immunosuppressant class encompasses antilymfocytární sérum, azaserin, basiliximab, cyklosporiny, daklizumab, elipticiny, everolimus, gliotoxin, koformycin, mycophenolic acid (kyselina mykofenolová), muromonab‑CD3, and tacrolimus (takrolimus). Production pathways of certain immunosuppressive preparations—specifically antilymfocytární sérum, azaserin and basiliximab—are directly linked to biosynthetic routes, underscoring the metabolic origins of these drugs. Within the immunosuppressant hierarchy, inhibitory interleukins form a child subgroup that dampens immune signaling. Because immunosuppressants reduce host immune defenses, their use is contraindicated in patients with active lidský herpesvirus 1 (human herpesvirus 1) infection, highlighting the need for infection screening before therapy. Understanding how these varied compounds qualify biosynthesis and how they intersect with immunosuppressive pharmacology is essential for optimizing therapeutic strategies, anticipating toxicological effects, and guiding patient care.

title: UMLS -- Run 25 -- Level 3 -- Community 1734
content: Antilymfocytární sérum, azaserin, basiliximab, cyklosporiny, daclizumab, everolimus, gliotoxin, koformycin, kyselina mykofenolová, muromonab‑CD3 and takrolimus are all classified as immunosuppressive agents. These drugs share the common function of dampening immune system activity, which makes them valuable in settings such as organ transplantation, where preventing graft rejection is essential, and in the treatment of certain autoimmune disorders. Antilymfocytární sérum and muromonab‑CD3 act as antibodies that target lymphocytes, while basiliximab, daclizumab and takrolimus inhibit T‑cell activation through distinct molecular pathways. Cyklosporiny and everolimus belong to the calcineurin‑inhibitor and mTOR‑inhibitor families, respectively, providing synergistic suppression of cellular immunity. Azaserin, gliotoxin, koformycin and kyselina mykofenolová (mycophenolic acid) exert their effects by interfering with nucleic acid synthesis or other intracellular processes critical for lymphocyte proliferation. Together, this diverse group of immunosuppressants offers clinicians multiple mechanisms to tailor therapy according to the specific immunologic needs of each patient, thereby improving graft survival and disease control while balancing the risk of infection and other adverse effects.
